item.title: Meaning of the second image
item.content:encoded:
imagine needing gears;
this post was made by transistor gang

Anyway this was a truly exceptional read with an interesting take on an age-old belief combined with modern technology and honestly, like someone said before, I'm surprised it hasnt been done before. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would the foundation need to make a deal with the man if they were just going to provide amnestics, plant an agent, and bury it anyway? That makes no sense

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's just my personal dislike of C++, but it seems somewhat out of place here. A Lisp dialect might be more appropriate, given Lisp's, uh, mystical reputation. That said, there is something pleasingly ritualistic to the cadence of the code sample (which would likely be lost in a deluge of parentheses if translated to a Lisp dialect)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. +1 for sure.

It's a very solid concept - I love the idea of using the Amish community to obscure a technology-based anomaly, and the interview log has some great, tight dialogue. Easy and enjoyable.

My favorite part is the ending. The idea that we got these people to willingly help bury the object on their property with minimal fuss, then anesthetize them to forget it ever happened is the perfect balance of "benevolent Foundation" and "greater good Foundation". Finding that balance is hard, so big ups Nico.

Here's my only gripe: Using the Amish community to contain this object doesn't feel fully justified as-is. These communities aren't totally isolated - technology-using individuals could still pass within that "range", potentially. Just burying it underground in the middle of Amish Country feels a bit… unsecured? Clever as heck, sure, but still not totally Foundation spec.

I feel like the actual anomaly itself needs a bit more oomph, or a lot less oomph. Either it's so benign that burying it is the most cost vs. threat effective option, or it's so dangerous that moving it poses far, far too much risk. As it is right now, some kinda' prototype Broken God recruitment beacon? Not quite enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amish Containment Procedures. I grew up near Bird-in-Hand and wholeheartedly approve

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not amazing, but I really love using the Amish as containment for something like this. Also, points for working SunnyClockwork art into an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Anomalous technology is contained by an Amish community" is such a brilliant idea that I'm baffled it apparently hasn't been done before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interaction with the Amish man really makes this work, without it there’s a few nice pieces but it’s done well and brings everything together. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hesitent upvote.

If I may ask, why SCP-5231?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://fc04.deviantart.net/fs70/i/2014/101/8/4/angel_of_the_church_by_sunnyparallax-d7e0nl9.jpg
https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/df/12_Semiconductor_Chip_is_made_of_Transistors.jpg
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Edward_VII_Memorial_Clock_Tower,_West_Parade,_Bexhill_(inside)_(2).jpg

thank you to my critters and special thanks to aismallard and roget who helped in the creation of the code section and news tidbit respectively.

the verse was taken from here and is by ihp.
